sections.header: Verses 1‐12 — Letters to All Israel: “Return to the LORD”

refs.note: Hezekiah's Passover recalls the original Egyptian Passover. The invitation to 'all Israel' — including the fallen northern kingdom — transforms a national festival into a reunification appeal.
Celebrating Passover in the second month instead of the first follows the precedent in Numbers for those who were ritually unclean at the appointed time — Hezekiah applies Mosaic flexibility.

The invitation 'Return to the LORD … and he will return to you' (v. 6) echoes Hosea's contemporary appeal to the northern kingdom. The Chronicler shows Hezekiah acting as a political vehicle for prophetic theology.

notes.note: The principle here applies universally: God’s governance is consistent and his responses are proportional to human faithfulness. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Chronicler’s distinctive theological vocabulary shapes the presentation of these events. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: Hezekiah sends letters throughout all Israel and Judah — including Ephraim and Manasseh — inviting everyone to a Passover in Jerusalem. The letters appeal: “Return to the LORD... he will not turn his face from you if you return to him.” Some northern couriers are mocked, but some from Asher, Manasseh, and Zebulun humble themselves and come. “In Judah the hand of God was on the people to give them unity of mind.” The Chronicler’s “all Israel” ideal is partially realised.

heb.paragraph: Dāraš is the Chronicler's signature verb — appearing more than 40 times in 1-2 Chronicles, far more frequently than in Kings. 'To seek the LORD' is the defining act of faithful kingship in the Chronicler's theology. Kings who seek (dāraš) prosper; kings who forsake (ʿāzaḇ) are abandoned. This binary drives the entire narrative.

notes.note: The Chronicler records this history not merely as political narrative but as theological instruction. Every event demonstrates God's sovereign governance of His people and His unwavering faithfulness to the Davidic covenant. The believing reader is meant to see in these events the pattern of God's dealings with His church in every age.

The Chronicler's perspective consistently emphasizes God's faithfulness to His covenant promises despite Israel's unfaithfulness. This dual theme — human failure and divine grace — is the heartbeat of all Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Rom 3:25-26).

The Chronicler wrote for a post-exilic community needing to understand their identity and hope. His message remains vital: God's promises to David are irrevocable, worship is the center of covenant life, and faithfulness — not circumstances — determines blessing. Believers today can take the same comfort: our God is faithful even when we are not (2 Tim 2:13).

sections.header: Verses 13‐27 — Passover Celebrated; Grace for the Unclean; Extended Joy

refs.note: The seven-day festival doubled to fourteen recalls Solomon's temple dedication (7:8–10), which also extended beyond the prescribed period. Extraordinary joy exceeds ritual boundaries.

The comparison to Solomon's celebration is made explicit: 'there was great joy in Jerusalem, for since the time of Solomon … there had been nothing like this' (v. 26). Hezekiah achieves a second Solomonic moment.

notes.note: Calvin: On 27 — Passover Celebrated; Grace for the Unclean; Extended Joy: teaches that all human religious activity is ultimately provisional — when God himself appears, our best efforts are suspended in awe. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: On 27 — Passover Celebrated; Grace for the Unclean; Extended Joy: Gen 22:2. Jewish tradition universally accepted this identification, which became foundational for Jerusalem’s theology of sacred space. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: A great assembly gathers. Many eat the Passover without being ceremonially clean. Hezekiah prays: “May the LORD, who is good, pardon everyone who sets their heart on seeking God — even if they are not clean.” God hears and heals the people. The celebration is so joyful they extend it seven more days. “There was great joy in Jerusalem, for since the days of Solomon there had been nothing like this.” The Chronicler places Hezekiah’s Passover second only to Solomon’s dedication.

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew ʿāzaḇ (to forsake) is the negative counterpart to dāraš (to seek). In the Chronicler's theology, abandoning God triggers a reciprocal abandonment: 'You abandoned me; therefore I abandon you' (2 Chr 12:5). This talionic principle — measure for measure — is the Chronicler's fundamental explanation for national disaster.

notes.note: Every genuine revival in Israel's history was rooted in a return to God's Word. The pattern is consistent: rediscovery of Scripture leads to repentance, which leads to reformation of worship. This remains the pattern for spiritual renewal in every age — the church is reformed and revived only as it returns to the authority of God's written Word.

The Chronicler's perspective consistently emphasizes God's faithfulness to His covenant promises despite Israel's unfaithfulness. This dual theme — human failure and divine grace — is the heartbeat of all Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Rom 3:25-26).

Every revival recorded in Scripture was initiated by God and sustained by obedience to His Word. The pattern is instructive for the modern church: spiritual renewal does not come through innovative programs or cultural accommodation but through humble return to the unchanging truth of Scripture. 'Will you not revive us again, that your people may rejoice in you?' (Ps 85:6).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVDo not be like your parents and your fellow Israelites, who were unfaithful to the Lord, the God of their ancestors, so that he made them an object of horror, as you see.ESVDo not be like your fathers and your brothers, who were faithless to the LORD God of their fathers, so that he made them a desolation, as you see.NIVThey decided to send a proclamation throughout Israel, from Beersheba to Dan, calling the people to come to Jerusalem and celebrate the Passover to the Lord, the God of Israel. It had not been celebrated in large numbers according to what was written.ESVSo they decreed to make a proclamation throughout all Israel, from Beersheba to Dan, that the people should come and keep the Passover to the LORD, the God of Israel, at Jerusalem, for they had not kept it as often as prescribed.
